title: Want to Think Better? Don't Ask AI for Help Right Away!
summary: Have you ever asked a chatbot to help with homework right away? A new study says you might want to think twice! Researchers at the University of Chicago asked 393 people to pretend they were city council members and decide on a water cleanup plan. Some could use an AI chatbot early, some later, and some not at all. The people who had 30 minutes to work and used the chatbot only after trying on their own wrote the best essays. They included more ideas and remembered more from the documents they read. But here's the twist: when people only had 10 minutes, those who used the chatbot from the start scored higher. So, if you're in a hurry, AI can help you speed up. But if you have time, it's better to struggle a bit first. That's because slow, careful thinking builds stronger understanding. "When you are under time pressure and use AI to boost your performance, then you are basically risking just taking the AI's framing," says researcher Mina Lee. So next time, try solving the problem yourself before you type that prompt!
why_it_matters: Next time you have a tricky homework question, wrestling with it on your own first might make you smarter, even if it feels harder than just asking a chatbot.

keywords.explanation: A group of people elected to make decisions and rules for a city.
The way an idea or problem is presented, which can shape how you think about it.
The text you type into an AI chatbot to ask for help or information.
Written papers that give information, used here as the background reading in the experiment.
How well you do something, like solving a problem or writing an essay.
A feeling of urgency or stress, often because time is running out.

questions.question: Who were the participants in the study pretending to be?
What was the task given in the experiment?
options: Write a story about a robot
Decide on a water cleanup plan
questions.correct_answer: Decide on a water cleanup plan

questions.question: Which group wrote the best essays and remembered more from the documents?
options: People who used AI from the very start with plenty of time
People who never used AI at all
People who tried on their own first and used AI later, when they had 30 minutes
People who only had 10 minutes and used AI early
questions.correct_answer: People who tried on their own first and used AI later, when they had 30 minutes

questions.question: When did using AI from the beginning help people score higher?
options: When they had lots of time
When they didn't read the documents

questions.question: What did researcher Mina Lee say you risk if you use AI under time pressure?
options: Taking the AI's framing instead of thinking for yourself
questions.correct_answer: Taking the AI's framing instead of thinking for yourself

questions.question: What is the main advice of this article?
options: Always use AI first to save time
If you have time, try to solve the problem yourself before asking AI
questions.correct_answer: If you have time, try to solve the problem yourself before asking AI

background_read: The University of Chicago is a famous university in the United States where many important studies about thinking and learning happen.
Mina Lee is a researcher who studies how people work with artificial intelligence and how it changes their decisions.
Scientists have found that struggling a little to learn something is actually good—it makes your brain stronger, an idea called 'desirable difficulty'.
Article_Structure: WHO: Researchers at the University of Chicago, including Mina Lee
WHAT: They found that using AI only after trying yourself leads to deeper understanding and better work, unless time is very short.
WHERE: In an experiment with 393 people making a pretend city council decision.
WHY: Because your own slow, hard thinking builds connections in your brain that simply asking a chatbot does not.

perspectives.description: Many students see AI as a quick fix, but they might miss out on really understanding the material and end up confused later.
Educators worry that if kids rely on AI right away, they won't build the critical thinking skills needed for harder subjects.
Researchers point out that in fast-paced jobs, AI can be a helpful tool, but using it wisely means knowing when to think for yourself first.
